We walked through extracting popup parameters using %param, reading selected instances via READ ENTITIES, and performing controlled updates using MODIFY ENTITIES in local mode. Finally, we ensured proper UI synchronization by returning the updated result set back to the Fiori Elements application. 

Thanks--

entry.title: The Hidden Cost of Custom Code in SAP IS U — And How BTP Reduces It Without a Big Bang
entry.content:
Executive Summary

For years, SAP IS‑U has quietly carried the weight of utility operations. Billing accuracy, regulatory compliance, and meter‑to‑cash stability always came first — and rightly so. But the side effect of that stability is a landscape full of Z‑programs, user exits, and enhancements that were built for good reasons at the time, yet now create friction when utilities try to modernize.

As the industry shifts toward decarbonization, DER growth, and real‑time customer expectations, the question isn’t “Is custom code a problem?” It’s “How do we reduce its impact without breaking the systems that keep the lights on?”

SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) offers a practical, low‑risk way to separate custom logic from the core and prepare for S/4HANA — without a disruptive rewrite or a multi‑year freeze.

1. The Custom Code Reality Inside SAP IS‑U

 Most custom code in IS‑U wasn’t a mistake. It was a necessity.

Utilities added enhancements to:

  • Meet state‑specific regulatory rules
  • Support complex rate structures
  • Integrate with legacy CIS, AMI, and market systems
  • Automate workflows long before APIs or event frameworks existed

Over time, this created:

  • Thousands of Z‑objects
  • Enhancements buried deep inside billing, device management, and FICA
  • Business logic tightly coupled to SAP’s upgrade cycle

What once delivered flexibility now slows down change.

2. The Hidden Costs Most Programs Underestimate

2.1 Upgrade and Migration Friction

Every enhancement inside the core increases:

  • SP and EHP regression testing effort
  • S/4HANA conversion complexity
  • Dependency on niche IS‑U technical expertise

Custom code does not just slow upgrades — it dictates upgrade timing.

2.2 Operational Risk

Many utilities still rely on:

  • Poorly documented enhancements
  • Knowledge concentrated in a few long‑tenured developers
  • Custom logic triggered deep inside critical transactions

This creates silent operational risk — especially during incident response or regulatory audits.

2.3 Innovation Tax

Each new digital initiative must answer an uncomfortable question:

“Do we modify the core again, or build around it?”

When the core is overloaded with custom logic, innovation slows — not because ideas are weak, but because change becomes expensive and risky.

3. Why ‘Rewrite Everything’ Is Not a Strategy

Most utilities cannot absorb:

  • A full custom code rewrite
  • A large‑scale CIS replacement
  • A multi‑year freeze on business change

A big‑bang approach often introduces more risk than it removes.

A more realistic path is selective decoupling — and that’s where BTP fits naturally.

4. How SAP BTP Reduces Custom Code Impact — Pragmatically

BTP gives utilities a way to shift custom logic out of the core while keeping IS‑U stable.

4.1 Keep the Core Clean — for Real

Instead of embedding logic in IS‑U:

  • Business rules move to BTP services
  • Extensions run side‑by‑side, not inside ECC or S/4HANA
  • Core processes remain upgrade‑friendly

4.2 Event‑Driven Extensions

Using events and APIs:

  • IS‑U triggers events (billing completion, device updates, move‑in/out)
  • BTP processes logic asynchronously
  • Failures are isolated, not system‑wide

4.3 Reusable Innovation Layer

Once on BTP, the same extension framework can support:

  • DER orchestration
  • AI‑based billing analytics
  • Customer self‑service enhancements
  • Regulatory reporting automation

This turns custom logic into assets, not liabilities.

5. Reference Architecture (Conceptual)

Below is a simplified conceptual architecture illustrating how SAP IS‑U and BTP coexist.

This model allows utilities to:

  • Retire high‑risk custom code gradually
  • Introduce new capabilities without touching the core
  • Prepare for S/4HANA on their own timeline

6. A Phased, Utility‑Friendly Adoption Model

Phase 1: Observe

  • Identify custom code hotspots
  • Monitor usage, performance, and change frequency

Phase 2: Isolate

  • Move non‑core logic to BTP extensions
  • Replace enhancements with APIs and events

Phase 3: Optimize

  • Retire unused Z‑objects
  • Standardize extension patterns
  • Accelerate future innovation

This approach delivers value within existing budgets and without destabilizing operations. 

7. Final Thought for Utility Leaders

The goal is not to eliminate custom code overnight. The goal is to stop letting yesterday’s decisions dictate tomorrow’s options.

SAP BTP provides a practical, low‑risk path to reduce technical debt, lower long‑term cost, and modernize SAP IS‑U — without a big bang and without compromising the core.

If you are evaluating S/4HANA readiness, IS‑U modernization, or clean‑core strategies, the conversation should start with architecture, not migration dates.

If you are evaluating S/4HANA readiness, IS‑U modernization, or clean‑core strategies, the conversation should start with architecture — not migration dates.

entry.title: ABAP Cloud Learning Map
entry.content:
The ABAP Cloud development model (ABAP Cloud) is the foundation of ABAP-based development at SAP, its partners, and customers, designed from the ground up to adhere to clean core principles. It brings together evolving technologies and methodologies into a sustainable, long-lasting programming model. 

The ABAP Cloud development model encompasses all aspects of modern cloud-ready software development, resulting in a wide range of topics that need to be understood. Therefore, clear guidance is helpful for getting started with and navigating ABAP Cloud.

Discover the ABAP Cloud Learning Map🗺️ – your guide to mastering the ABAP Cloud development model. It provides an overview of essential topics and skills, along with a curated collection of links to key resources covering the technologies, tools, approaches, and practices that drive cloud-ready, upgrade-stable ABAP development. 📚Whether you’re starting out or filling knowledge gaps, the learning map helps you navigate modern ABAP Cloud development at your own pace. 

🔗Access it here: ABAP Cloud Learning Map↗

entry.title: Week 1 : Set up the Basic Trial account enabled with Joule for Developers
entry.content:
The Basic Trial offering for SAP Build has been extended with a new workbook called 'Fusion Development with ABAP Cloud in SAP Build'. You can explore this workbook for the Basic Trial offering around SAP Build, that shows how developers can now create new ABAP Cloud projects, or work on existing ones, from the SAP Build lobby and then navigate to their ABAP development environment – the ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse (ADT).

Task 1 : Set up the Basic Trial account and add the ABAP instance in ADT.

Click the following URL:   

https://www.sap.com/products/technology-platform/build/trial.html

In the following screen click on "Try Now". 

You will receive a user with a password that will allow you to authenticate at the landing page of the basic trial in the respective region for your user, e.g. https://trials.cfapps.eu10-004.hana.ondemand.com/ .  

Basic Trial Registration

By using the credentials presented in the web page you will get access to some workbooks that cover different scenarios. 

The ABAP related workbook is named Fusion Development with ABAP Cloud in SAP Build.

Select the highlighted workbook.

Choose the Highlighted Workbook

Click ‘Start Workbook’

Start WorkBook

Note : It may take up to 30 minutes to fully activate your user account in the SAP BTP ABAP Environment. 

Read the details mentioned in Overview of the Section ‘Set up your development environment’. You can skip Step 1 and 2 if you already have ADT installed but make sure you are on the latest version of ADT for all the features to work fine. Once the installation is done directly move to Step 3 - 'Create ABAP Cloud Project in SAP Build' only until adding the basic trial instance to ADT. We will be it using the URL marked in the below screenshot.

Note:- Once you have the system added in ADT you can close the workbook.

System URL

Task 2 :  Create a package in the new ABAP Cloud Project 
Once you see the new ABAP Cloud Project (TDI ) added in ADT, create a package with the naming convention ZAC_<your community ID>.

Note: Please make sure you are using the latest version of ADT, otherwise package creation will not work.

To get the community ID launch the URL : https://community.sap.com/ and click on the person icon as shown in below screenshot and go to ‘My Content’.

Community Page

Once the below page opens up please copy the number after user-id in the URL to get your community ID.

Community ID

Note:- We will be validating your activities by checking the folder against your community ID. So make sure all your development objects are saved inside this package.

Task 3 :  Learn about the SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI Capabilities

To prepare for the challenge familiarise yourself with the scope first by going through the following help document.

https://help.sap.com/docs/abap-cloud/abap-development-tools-user-guide/joule-for-developers-abap-ai-capabilities-f14ebffef77b41bfb0746c33dcb70e84?locale=en-US

Now you are ready to take up the challenge. See you next week !!

entry.title: ABAP Developer Challenge - Joule for Developers using Basic Trial
entry.content:
Let’s kick off this year’s Developer Challenge with a topic close to home: ABAP.

With AI dominating conversations across the tech world, we thought it would be exciting for our community to explore Joule for Developers this month.

SAP Joule for Developers brings embedded AI capabilities to SAP Build and ABAP, delivered through Joule. It helps accelerate the development of apps and extensions, reduce development costs, and enhance overall developer productivity and proficiency.

SAP Joule for Developers is engineered for ABAP excellence by

  • Leveraging SAP’s ABAP large language model trained on enterprise-grade code.
  • Generating data models, app logic, and unit tests with precision.
  • Migrating custom ABAP code faster with documentation chat, code explanation, and issue diagnostics.

The Challenge:

  For this challenge we will be using Basic Trial offering for SAP Build that includes a SAP BTP ABAP Environment with Joule for Developers. During the first week we will focus on setting up the ABAP instance from the Basic Trial in ADT and then will explore some of the key functionalities of Joule for Developers.

How to Participate:

To participate in and complete this challenge, simply follow these steps:

  • Complete the challenge assigned for each week.
  • Create a package with your community ID in the format mentioned in the Week1 challenge.
  • Post your package name in the thread for each week.
  • We will review your work inside the package with your community ID.

Challenge Timeline

The challenge will span for 4 weeks starting on Monday March 2nd 2026 and ending on March 23rd 2026. We will keep the challenge open till the end of the month to accomodate anyone who is running late 🙂. To count towards the overall monthly challenge, you must complete and submit each week's task. Make sure to revisit this blog post to discover the upcoming week's challenge!

March 2nd, 2026   – Start of Challenge: Week 1

March 9th, 2026    –  Week 2 

March 16th, 2026  – Week 3 :

March 23rd, 2026  – Week 4 :

So let’s get started.

Rewards

Upon submitting all required entries by the end of the challenge, you'll earn an SAP Community badge for participating in the SAP Developer Challenge for March. Please ensure to follow all the instructions mentioned in this blogpost or the respective challenge discussions.

We are excited to hear your experience about the challenge and the learning adventure.

Best of Luck !!!

entry.title: new way to create destination from BAS
entry.content:
Destination creation from BAS

Introduction : A destination in SAP BTP isa secure configuration object used to define connection details for outbound communication from BTP applications to remote services or systems, whether they are on-premise or cloud-based. They store essential technical information—such as URLs, authentication credentials, and proxy types—to enable seamless, secure integration.

Step1 : Open bas in btp 

Step2:open BAS, then you can see sidebar at left in that click on three dots then you can see service center option 

(service center in SAP system (that is connected to your trial account)) 

Step3:after clicking that you can see add system option 

Step4: provide the details of system like(system name, description, url, system type, client, proxy type, user name, password ) 

Step5: click on add then you can see message like hana2026 has been created 

Step6: come back to BTP subaccount and cross check the destination whether it is created or not 

Conclusion : Here is the new way to create destination in Business technology platform through business application studio, using service center option in business application studio we can create destination from BTP also using destination option in sap BTP sub account,

entry.title: A New System-Based ABAP Certification Is Here - What Developers Need to Know
entry.content:
After a long wait, it’s finally here: a new practical ABAP certification exam designed to test what really matters. This time, you’ll be working hands-on in a live SAP system.

🚀SAP Certified - Backend Developer - ABAP Cloud (Beta Version)

 If you’re planning to take it, here’s what you should know - and how to prepare effectively.

💡Good to know!

The new exam https://learning.sap.com/certifications/sap-certified-associate-back-end-developer-abap-cloud focuses on solving practical development tasks based on the content of:

  • Basic ABAP Programming (S4D400)
  • Intermediate ABAP Programming (S4D401)
  • Data Modelling in ABAP Dictionary and ABAP Core Data Services (S4D430)

You’ll work in an ABAP environment on SAP BTP, using the ABAP language version for cloud development.

If your background is primarily on-premise ABAP, this is a mindset shift.

 ℹ️We’re currently in the Beta phase of the certification exam.

What that means for you:

  •  3-hour exam instead of the planned 4-hour version
  • Same full certification as the final release - no “beta” label
  • Early opportunity before the scope expands

 

After Beta, the exam will be extended to 4 hours with broader coverage.

Don’t work harder, work smarter. Secure your spot now!

 

The Environment – ABAP Cloud

ABAP Cloud promotes:

  • Model-driven development
  • Use of Core Data Services (CDS)
  • ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP)
  • Automatic exposure of services (OData/Web APIs)
    All leading to faster, more scalable apps and services. 

🤓Hint: Before you start, be sure to update your Eclipse and ADT installations to the latest versions.

 

Why Practice Systems Are Critical

The certification system will resemble the training and practice systems available in learning environments - but it won’t be identical.

 The practice systems, including the exercise handbooks and system setup guides to practice and prepare, can be found here:

  • Basic ABAP Programming S4D400
  • Intermediate ABAP Programming S4D401
  • Data Modelling in ABAP Dictionary and ABAP Core Data Services S4D430

🤓Hint: Please refer to the course file that is downloaded when clicking the access button for further log on information

 

 Preparation is Essential.

 The practice systems give you access to:

  • The same underlying data model used in the exam
  • Identical table and field names
  • A comparable development setup

 Getting familiar with the structure of the data model and working with ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse (ADT) will save you valuable time during the exam.

 ⏲ And time matters!

You’ll have three hours to complete the exam.  Three hours go faster than you think!

🤓Hint: Joule for Developers is available within the exam system. We recommend using it while working through the tasks to help you complete them more efficiently.

 

 How to Prepare

Along with the practice Systems mentioned above, the Learning Journey  Acquiring Core ABAP Skills will help you to prepare for your exam.

 

Good Luck 🍀 and Happy Learning!

 

For more information on the Reimagine Certification initiative, please read: Reimagining Certification for the Way We Work Toda... - SAP Community

 

SAP Learning Services Topic Page 
Ask questions about Training and follow 
Read other Training blog posts and follow

entry.title: How to Connect On-Premise ABAP System to SAP BTP (Business Application Studio)-BAS
entry.content:
SAP BTP (Business Application Studio):

In modern SAP architectures, customers build Fiori, RAP, and CAP applications on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) while keeping their ERP systems on-premise. To enable secure communication between SAP BTP and an on-premise ABAP system.

Prerequisites:

 1.SAP BTP Subaccount (Trial or Enterprise).

2.SAP Cloud Connector Installed.

3.On-Premises ABAP System Details.

Important:
Cloud Connector establishes an outbound encrypted connection. No inbound firewall ports are required.
Step-by-Step Configuration: Create Destination in BTP.
Step 1: Open SAP BTP Cockpit:  Open your browser and navigate to:  https://cockpit.hanatrial.ondemand.com/trial/#/home/trial   

This blog does not cover how to create a SAP BTP account, as many videos and tutorials and official SAP documentation already explain that process in detail.

Before proceeding, ensure:

  • You have access to an SAP BTP Global Account
  • A Subaccount is already created
  • Connectivity and Destination services are enabled
  • SAP Business Application Studio is subscribed

Once these prerequisites are completed, continue with the Cloud Connector configuration.

 Click on Go To Your Trail Account:

 After That Click On   Trail Button.

Click On Destination Button:

Click on Create Button:

 Select From Scratch Option and Provide  to   The Your On-Premises ABAP System   Detail:

Note: Username and Password  should be your On-Premise ABAP System

Example: 

Note:  While creating the Destination in SAP BTP, make sure the URL field is correctly.

  • Your Virtual Host name
  • The Port number
  • The correct protocol prefix
    Example: http://nayakhana.com:8899

The http:// prefix is mandatory.

After That Please click on Add Property  and Give to the Below Details .

Note:    Hear sap-client---> 100 means this  is  your On-Premise ABAP System Client Number.

Example:

Once you have maintained all required details of your On-Premise ABAP system in the Destination configuration:

  • URL (Virtual Host + Port)
  • Proxy Type = OnPremise
  • Authentication = BasicAuthentication
  • Backend Username
  • Backend Password
  • Additional Properties (sap-client, etc.)
  • Click on “Check Connection”.

If you have maintained all configuration details correctly in  SAP BTP Destination, and you click Check Connection, you may see the message:"Backend is reachable from the Cloud Connector".

This confirms that the first step of BTP Destination connectivity is completed successfully.

Please Go  to The  Below link For:

 Step 2-  SAP Cloud Connector.
https://community.sap.com/t5/abap-blog-posts/how-to-connect-sap-cloud-connector-to-sap-btp/ba-p/143… 
Step 3:   Connect BAS To  SAP On-Premise System:

https://community.sap.com/t5/abap-blog-posts/connect-sap-business-application-studio-bas-to-on-premise-abap-system/ba-p/14340482 



Thank You,

B Naresh Kumar Nayak.

entry.title: IoT: Air Quality Dashboard with SAP RAP and Mobile App
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community!

After my last blog about IoT and SAP CAP, I’d like to share a new pet project combining IoT and SAP ABAP RAP.
Last summer was extremely hot, and I kept wondering when it would be best to open the windows in our meeting room to get some fresh air. That led me to another question: could I measure the air quality and integrate the data with SAP Cloud Platform?
Elevated concentrations of carbon dioxide (CO2) and insufficient ventilation in indoor environments can decrease cognitive performance and may cause headaches. CO2 detectors are used to monitor concentrations and provide a warning when predefined thresholds are exceeded. Carbon dioxide concentration is expressed in ppm (parts per million) which represents the number of carbon dioxide molecules per one million molecules of air.

Indoor air quality is classified according to the following concentration ranges:

  • at concentration below 800ppm, air quality is considered good;
  • between 800 and 1000ppm, slight stuffiness may be perceived;
  • between 1000 and 1500ppm, air quality is poor and cognitive performance may decrease;
  • between 1500 and 2000ppm, headaches and drowsiness may occur, and ventilation is recommended;
  • above 2000ppm indicate significantly elevated CO2 levels and require immediate ventilation to improve indoor air quality;

So, I decided to build the IoT device on my favorite Raspberry Pi platform. For this project, I chose the fourth-generation Raspberry Pi 4 single-board computer.
Also I found a CO₂ sensor that produces an analog voltage signal. To process this data digitally, it first needs to be converted using an analog-to-digital converter (ADC).

1.Hardware part

Below are the links to the devices I used:

CO2 Sensor SKU SEN0159 

DFRobot I2C ADS1115 

All components are connected.

 

The GPIO shows which wire connects to which pin:

Actually, it looks as follows:

After many tries, I combined the example programs from both devices into a Python script to test the sensor.

A quick (and possibly boring) theory about the calibration curve:

The sensor outputs a voltage that decreases as CO₂ concentration increases. To convert voltage to ppm, we use a logarithmic calibration curve:

Where:

  • log_400 = 2.602 → log10(400 ppm), the baseline for clean air.
  • V_zero = ZERO_POINT_VOLTAGE
  • slope = REACTION_VOLTAGE / (log_400 - log_1000)

This allows us to map measured voltages to actual CO₂ concentrations in ppm.

import time
import math
import sys
import os

sys.path.append(os.path.dirname(os.path.dirname(os.path.realpath(__file__))))
from DFRobot_ADS1115 import ADS1115

# Device parameters
MG_PIN = 0  # define which analog input channel you are going to user
DC_GAIN = 8.5  # define the DC gain of amplifier

# Programm parameters
READ_SAMPLE_INTERVAL = 0.05  # Interval between measurements (sec.)
READ_SAMPLE_TIMES = 5  # Sample numbers

# Calibration
ZERO_POINT_VOLTAGE = 0.2936  # Default street voltage for 400ppm
REACTION_VOLTAGE = 0.030  # Voltage drop 1000ppm
CO2Curve = [2.602, ZERO_POINT_VOLTAGE, (REACTION_VOLTAGE / (2.602 - 3))]

# Initialization ADS1115
ads1115 = ADS1115()
ads1115.set_addr_ADS1115(0x48)  # Set I2C adress
ads1115.set_gain(0x00)  # Set gain 6.144V

def read_adc(channel):
    """Reading ADS1115"""
    adc_val = ads1115.read_voltage(channel)
    return adc_val['r'] / 1000.0  # Transform to volts

def mg_read(mg_pin):
    """Reading average voltage"""
    v = 0
    for _ in range(READ_SAMPLE_TIMES):
        v += read_adc(mg_pin)
        time.sleep(READ_SAMPLE_INTERVAL)
    v = v / READ_SAMPLE_TIMES
    return v

def mg_get_percentage(volts, pcurve):
    """Measurement CO₂ concentration в ppm"""
    if (volts / DC_GAIN) >= ZERO_POINT_VOLTAGE:
        return -1
    else:
        return 10 ** (((volts / DC_GAIN) - pcurve[1]) / pcurve[2] + pcurve[0])

def ppm_to_percent(ppm):
    """Calculate ppm to percents"""
    return ppm / 10000.0  # 1 ppm = 0.0001%

if __name__ == "__main__":
    print("MG-811 Demo")
    try:
        while True:
            volts = mg_read(MG_PIN)
            print(f"SEN0159: {volts:.3f}V", end="  ")
            
            percentage = mg_get_percentage(volts, CO2Curve)
            if percentage == -1:
                print("CO2: <400 ppm (<0.04%)")
            else:
                percent = ppm_to_percent(percentage)
                print(f"CO2: {int(percentage)} ppm ({percent:.4f}%)")
            
            time.sleep(0.5)
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        print("Program terminated by user")

Key Parameters from the script:

  • MG_PIN = 0 – Analog input channel for the MG-811 sensor.
  • DC_GAIN = 8.5 – Amplifier gain of the sensor.
  • READ_SAMPLE_INTERVAL = 0.05 s – Time between measurements.
  • READ_SAMPLE_TIMES = 5 – Number of samples to average.
  • ZERO_POINT_VOLTAGE = 0.2936 V – Voltage at 400 ppm CO₂ (clean air).
  • REACTION_VOLTAGE = 0.030 V – Voltage drop at 1000 ppm CO₂.

To determine ZERO_POINT_VOLTAGE, I put the sensor outside in clean air.

2. Backend part

I skipped the creation of the SAP BTP Trial user, ABAP Environment, and ABAP Cloud project in Eclipse. Please use the links below.

Get an Account on SAP BTP Trial 

Create an SAP BTP ABAP Environment Trial User 

Create an ABAP Cloud Project 

Let’s focus on creating OData service.

Create a package:

…and inside create a database table:

Fieldname

Data type

Description

Client

Abap.clnt

Client

Id

Abap.int4

Unique ID

Device

Abap.char(50)

Device name

Voltage

Abap.dec(15.5)

Voltage

PPM

Abap.int4

PPM

Percent

Abap.dec(10.5)

Percent

Created_at

Abap.utclong

Timestamp for create

Updated_at

Abap.utclong

Timestamp for change

@EndUserText.label : 'Sensor data'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table ztsensordat_giv {

  key client : abap.clnt not null;
  key id     : abap.int4 not null;
  device     : abap.char(50);
  voltage    : abap.dec(15,5);
  ppm        : abap.int4;
  percent    : abap.dec(10,5);
  created_at : abap.utclong;
  updated_at : abap.utclong;

}

Generate OData Web Service API after database table activation.

The following objects will be generated as part of an ABAP RAP managed scenario.

After generation and activation, I made a small change to enable auto-increment for the ID field and automatic timestamp insertion. For the Behavior Definition I enable “early numbering” and set “readonly” for the fields ID, CreatedAt, ChangedAt.

managed implementation in class ZBP_R_TSENSORDAT_GIV unique;
strict ( 2 );
extensible;
define behavior for ZR_TSENSORDAT_GIV alias ZrTsensordatGiv
persistent table ZTSENSORDAT_GIV
extensible
lock master
authorization master( global )
early numbering
{

  field ( readonly ) Id;
  field ( readonly ) CreatedAt;
  field ( readonly ) UpdatedAt;

  create;
  update;
  delete;

determination set_created_at on save { create; }
determination set_updated_at on modify { update; }

  mapping for ZTSENSORDAT_GIV corresponding extensible
  {
    ID = ID;
    Device = DEVICE;
    Voltage = VOLTAGE;
    Ppm = PPM;
    Percent = PERCENT;
    CreatedAt = CREATED_AT;
    UpdatedAt = UPDATED_AT;
  }
}

In the class referenced in the Behavior Definition (Local Types), implement logic for auto-increment and timestamp handling.

CLASS lhc_zr_tsensordat_giv DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_handler.
  PRIVATE SECTION.

    METHODS earlynumbering_create FOR NUMBERING
      IMPORTING entities FOR CREATE ZrTsensordatGiv.

    METHODS get_global_authorizations FOR GLOBAL AUTHORIZATION
      IMPORTING REQUEST requested_authorizations FOR ZrTsensordatGiv RESULT result.

    METHODS set_created_at FOR DETERMINE ON SAVE
      IMPORTING keys FOR ZrTsensordatGiv~set_created_at.

    METHODS set_updated_at FOR DETERMINE ON MODIFY
      IMPORTING keys FOR ZrTsensordatGiv~set_updated_at.

ENDCLASS.

CLASS lhc_zr_tsensordat_giv IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD earlynumbering_create.

    DATA: entity TYPE STRUCTURE FOR CREATE Zr_Tsensordat_Giv,
          id_max TYPE ztsensordat_giv-id.

    " Copy entities w/o ID
    LOOP AT entities INTO entity WHERE id IS NOT INITIAL.
      APPEND CORRESPONDING #( entity ) TO mapped-zrtsensordatgiv.
    ENDLOOP.

    DATA(entities_wo_id) = entities.
    entities_wo_id[] = entities[].

    DELETE entities_wo_id WHERE Id IS NOT INITIAL.

    " Determine max ID
    SELECT MAX( Id ) FROM ztsensordat_giv INTO _max.

    LOOP AT entities_wo_id INTO entity.
      id_max += 1.
      entity-Id = id_max.

      entity-createdat = utclong_current( ).

      " Adding to the table
      APPEND CORRESPONDING #( entity ) TO mapped-zrtsensordatgiv.

      APPEND VALUE #( %cid = entity-%cid
                      %key = entity-%key ) TO mapped-zrtsensordatgiv.
    ENDLOOP.

  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD get_global_authorizations.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD set_created_at.

    READ ENTITIES OF zr_tsensordat_giv IN LOCAL MODE
    ENTITY zrtsensordatgiv
    FIELDS ( CreatedAt UpdatedAt )
    WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys )
    RESULT DATA(lt_sensordat).

    LOOP AT lt_sensordat ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_sensordat>).
      IF <fs_sensordat>-CreatedAt IS INITIAL.
        <fs_sensordat>-CreatedAt = utclong_current( ).
      ELSE.
        <fs_sensordat>-UpdatedAt = utclong_current( ).
      ENDIF.
    ENDLOOP.

    MODIFY ENTITIES OF zr_tsensordat_giv IN LOCAL MODE
      ENTITY zrtsensordatgiv
      UPDATE FIELDS ( CreatedAt UpdatedAt )
      WITH CORRESPONDING #( lt_sensordat ).

  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD set_updated_at.

  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

Activate and publish the OData service.

Before testing the service, I create a class for test data generation:

CLASS zcl_insert_data_sensordat DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_oo_adt_classrun.
  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    METHODS generate_random_data
      IMPORTING iv_count TYPE i.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_insert_data_sensordat IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD if_oo_adt_classrun~main.
    DATA(lv_count) = 10.

    DELETE FROM ztsensordat_giv.

    generate_random_data( iv_count = lv_count ).

    COMMIT WORK.

    out->write( |[RAP] { lv_count } random entries have inserted into ZTSENSORDAT_GIV.| ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD generate_random_data.
    DATA: ls_sensordat_giv TYPE ztsensordat_giv,
          lv_time          TYPE utclong,
          lv_index         TYPE i,
          lv_voltage       TYPE f,
          lv_ppm           TYPE i,
          lv_percent       TYPE f.

    " Generators initialization
    DATA(lo_rand_int)   = cl_abap_random_int=>create( seed = cl_abap_random=>seed( ) ).
    DATA(lo_rand_float) = cl_abap_random_float=>create( seed = cl_abap_random=>seed( ) ).

    lv_time = utclong_current( ).

    DO iv_count TIMES.
      lv_index = sy-index.

      " Random generation
      lv_voltage = conv f( '1.5' ) + lo_rand_float->get_next( ) * conv f( '3.0' ). 
      lv_ppm     = 400 + lo_rand_int->get_next( ) MOD 601. " from 400 to 1000
      lv_percent = lv_ppm / 10000 * 100.              

      " Fill the table structure
      ls_sensordat_giv-id         = lv_index.
      ls_sensordat_giv-device     = |DEMO_SENSOR_{ lv_index }|.
      ls_sensordat_giv-voltage    = lv_voltage.
      ls_sensordat_giv-ppm        = lv_ppm.
      ls_sensordat_giv-percent    = lv_percent.
      ls_sensordat_giv-created_at = lv_time.
      ls_sensordat_giv-updated_at = lv_time.

      INSERT ztsensordat_giv FROM _sensordat_giv.
    ENDDO.
  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

Running the class will populate the table with random test data

Now we can test data retrieval.

Perfect, OData service works. At least for reading.

For the safe communication outside of SAP BTP platform, we need to create a XSUAA.

XSUAA (XS User Account and Authentication) is the authentication and authorization service of SAP Business Technology Platform.
It provides secure access to applications by issuing OAuth 2.0 JWT tokens, managing scopes and roles, and integrating with SAP Identity Authentication Service or external identity providers.
XSUAA is commonly used to protect APIs, applications, and service-to-service communication on SAP BTP.

We can find the service at Service Marketplace by name Authorization and Service management. We create an instance and service key.

The parameters will be defined in a JSON file.

{
  "xsappname": "xsuaa_iot",
  "tenant-mode": "dedicated",
  "scopes": [
    {
      "name": "$XSAPPNAME.Viewer",
      "description": "Read access to sensor data"
    },
    {
      "name": "$XSAPPNAME.Admin",
      "description": "Admin access to sensor data"
    }
  ],
  "role-templates": [
    {
      "name": "Viewer",
      "description": "Read-Only Role",
      "scope-references": ["$XSAPPNAME.Viewer"]
    },
    {
      "name": "Admin",
      "description": "Full Access Role",
      "scope-references": ["$XSAPPNAME.Admin"]
    }
  ],
  "oauth2-configuration": {
    "grant-types": [
      "password", 
      "authorization_code", 
      "client_credentials", 
      "refresh_token"
    ]
  },
  "authorities": []
}

Main parameters for the JSON are:

xsappname
Defines the technical name of the XSUAA application.
It is used as a namespace for scopes and roles.

“xsuaa_lot”

tenant-mode
Specifies whether the application is single-tenant or multi-tenant.
dedicated means one tenant per application instance.

“dedicated”

 scopes
Define what actions are allowed in the application.
Scopes are later included in OAuth JWT tokens.

“Viewer”, “Admin”

role-templates
Group scopes into business roles.
These roles are assigned to users via role collections.

oauth2-configuration
Defines which OAuth 2.0 grant types the application supports,
for example user login or service-to-service communication.

How tokens can be obtained.

  • authorization_code
    Used for interactive user login.
    Typical flow for UI applications like SAP Fiori or UI5, where the user is redirected to log in.
  • password
    Used when an application directly exchanges username and password for a token.
    Mainly for legacy or technical scenarios (generally discouraged today).
  • client_credentials
    Used for service-to-service communication.
    No user involved — the application authenticates itself.
  • refresh_token
    Allows the application to refresh an access token without forcing the user to log in again.

Authorities
Used to reference external authorities or technical privileges.

For more details, see the following link:

Application Security Descriptor Configuration Syntax 

I simplified this part and chose OAuth 2.0 Password authentication.

Once XSUAA instance is created, the respective XSUAA roles should be assigned to the technical user in the SAP BTP.

To connect to an SAP BTP OData service and send data, we first obtain a user token. With this token, we then request a CSRF token, which is used to securely send sensor data to the database.

CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) is a security mechanism that protects the backend from unauthorized data modifications. It ensures that write operations, such as POST or PUT requests, are performed only by trusted clients.

With the CSRF token, we can send requests to the OData service.

The following Python script reads data from the CO₂ sensor and sends it to the OData service.

import requests
import json
import time
import math
import sys
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

# XSUAA credentials
load_dotenv()

client_id = os.getenv("CLIENT_ID")
client_secret = os.getenv("CLIENT_SECRET")
token_url = os.getenv("TOKEN_URL")
odata_url = os.getenv("ODATA_URL")
odata_post_url = os.getenv("ODATA_GET_URL")

# User credentials
username = os.getenv("USERNAME")
password = os.getenv("PASSWORD")

# Device parameters
MG_PIN = 0  # define which analog input channel you are going to use
DC_GAIN = 8.5  # define the DC gain of amplifier

# Programm parameters
READ_SAMPLE_INTERVAL = 0.05  # Interval between measurements (sec.)
READ_SAMPLE_TIMES = 5  # Sample numbers

# Calibration
ZERO_POINT_VOLTAGE = 0.2936  # Default street voltage 400ppm
REACTION_VOLTAGE = 0.030  # Voltage drop 1000ppm
CO2Curve = [2.602, ZERO_POINT_VOLTAGE, (REACTION_VOLTAGE / (2.602 - 3))]

# Initialization ADS1115
sys.path.append(os.path.dirname(os.path.dirname(os.path.realpath(__file__))))
from DFRobot_ADS1115 import ADS1115
ads1115 = ADS1115()
ads1115.set_addr_ADS1115(0x48)  # Set I2C address
ads1115.set_gain(0x00)  # Set gain 6.144V

# Function to read the ADC value
def read_adc(channel):
    """Reading ADS1115"""
    adc_val = ads1115.read_voltage(channel)
    return adc_val['r'] / 1000.0  # Transform to volts

# Function to read the average voltage from the sensor
def mg_read(mg_pin):
    """Reading average voltage"""
    v = 0
    for _ in range(READ_SAMPLE_TIMES):
        v += read_adc(mg_pin)
        time.sleep(READ_SAMPLE_INTERVAL)
    v = v / READ_SAMPLE_TIMES
    return v

# Function to get CO₂ percentage based on voltage
def mg_get_percentage(volts, pcurve):
    """Measurement CO₂ concentration in ppm"""
    if (volts / DC_GAIN) >= ZERO_POINT_VOLTAGE:
        return -1
    else:
        return 10 ** (((volts / DC_GAIN) - pcurve[1]) / pcurve[2] + pcurve[0])

# Function to convert ppm to percentage
def ppm_to_percent(ppm):
    """Calculate ppm to percent"""
    return ppm / 10000.0  # 1 ppm = 0.0001%

# 1. Получение токена пользователя
session = requests.Session()

# 1. Getting User Token
token_response = session.post(
    token_url,
    data={
        "grant_type": "password",
        "username": username,
        "password": password,
        "client_id": client_id,
        "client_secret": client_secret
    },
    headers={"Content-Type": "application/x-www-form-urlencoded"}
)

if token_response.status_code == 200:
    access_token = token_response.json().get("access_token")
    print("✅ User Token received")
else:
    print("❌ Token error:", token_response.status_code, token_response.text)
    exit()

# 2. Get CSRF Token

headers = {
    "Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}",
    "X-CSRF-Token": "Fetch"
}

# Make a GET request to fetch the CSRF token
csrf_response = session.get(url=odata_url, headers=headers)

if csrf_response.status_code == 200:
    csrf_token = csrf_response.headers.get("X-CSRF-Token")
    print("✅ CSRF Token received:", csrf_token)
else:
    print("❌ Error getting CSRF Token:", csrf_response.status_code, csrf_response.text)
    exit()

# 2. OData API call URL
headers = {
    "Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}",
    "Content-Type": "application/json",
    "Accept": "application/json",
    "X-CSRF-Token": csrf_token
}

# Set the interval for data sending (in seconds)
interval = 5  # по умолчанию 5 секунд

# Main loop to send data in a loop
try:
    while True:

        volts = mg_read(MG_PIN)
        voltage = volts
        #voltage = volts
        print(f"SEN0159: {volts:.3f}V", end="  ")

        percentage = mg_get_percentage(volts, CO2Curve)
        if percentage == -1:
            co2_value = 400
            percent = ppm_to_percent(co2_value)
            print("CO2: <400 ppm (<0.04%)")
        else:
            co2_value = int(percentage)
            percent = ppm_to_percent(co2_value)
            print(f"CO2: {co2_value} ppm ({percent:.4f}%)")
        
#        percent = f"{percent:.5f}"
        
#        print("Volts:", volts)
#        print("Voltage:", voltage)
#        print("CO2 value:", co2_value)
#        print("Percent:", percent)
        
        # Prepare the data for sending
        new_record = {
            "Device": "DFROBOT CO2 Sensor",
            "Voltage": round(volts,5),
            "Ppm": co2_value,
            "Percent": round(percent,5),
        }

#        # Prepare data for the new record (example)
#        new_record = {
#            "Device": "PYTHON_CO2_SENSOR",
#            "Voltage": 2.0100,
#            "Ppm": 1602,
#            "Percent": 0.1602,
#        }

        # Send POST request to add a new record
        response = session.post(url=odata_post_url, headers=headers, data=json.dumps(new_record))

        # Check the response
        print("Code:", response.status_code)
        print("Server response:", response.text)

        if response.status_code == 200 or response.status_code == 201:
            try:
#                print(response.json())
                print("✅ Successfully sent.")
            except requests.exceptions.JSONDecodeError:
                print("❌ Error. No JSON response.")
        else:
            print("❌ Error response:", response.status_code, response.text)

        # Wait before sending the next request
        time.sleep(interval)

except KeyboardInterrupt:
    print("Program terminated by user")

I placed the URLs and other parameters to .env file:

CLIENT_ID is taken from XSUAA service key

CLIENT_SECRET – from XSUAA service key

TOKEN_URL – from XSUAA service key

ODATA_URL – is taken from service URL, but for external non WEB access remove “-web” from the address

ODATA_GET_URL – is taken from the service URL + setting the entity set.

After execution, the Raspberry Pi begins sending data to the database table.

Our backend is ready.

 The backend part is ready, now let's move on to the frontend stage:

Instructions for creating a dev space for the Fiori application are available at the following link:

Create a Dev Space for SAP Fiori Apps 

Create a Dev Space for SAP Fiori:

Choose SAP Fiori generator:

…and choose

Let’s create a single page with a tile that displays CO₂ sensor data.

To make the OData service visible in the system, perform the following steps:

Login to CloudFoundry.

 

The following are the controller and view files:

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller"
], (Controller) => {
    "use strict";

    return Controller.extend("co2project.controller.Main", {

            onInit: function () {
              this._oModel = this.getOwnerComponent().getModel();
              this._oNumericContent = this.byId("numContent");
              this._oLevelText = this.byId("levelText");
              this._oSwitch = this.byId("autoRefreshSwitch");
        
              if (!this._oModel) {
                console.error("Model is not found");
              } else {
                this._loadLatestData();
        
              // Autorefresh every 10 sec (10000 мс)
              if (this._oSwitch.getState()) {
                this._startAutoRefresh();
                }      
        
              }
            },
        
            _startAutoRefresh: function () {
              this._refreshInterval = setInterval(function () {
                this._loadLatestData();
              }.bind(this), 10000); // every 10 sec
            },
            
            _stopAutoRefresh: function () {
              if (this._refreshInterval) {
                clearInterval(this._refreshInterval);
                this._refreshInterval = null;
              }
            },
        
            onAutoRefreshChange: function (oEvent) {
              var bState = oEvent.getParameter("state");
              if (bState) {
                this._startAutoRefresh();
                MessageToast.show("Autorefresh ON");
              } else {
                this._stopAutoRefresh();
                MessageToast.show("Autorefresh OFF");
              }
            },
        
            _loadLatestData: function () {
              var oSorter = new sap.ui.model.Sorter("CreatedAt", true);
              var oListBinding = this._oModel.bindList("/ZC_TSENSORDAT_GIV", undefined, [oSorter], undefined, {
                parameters: {
                  $orderby: "CreatedAt desc",
                  $top: 1
                }
              });
            
              oListBinding.attachDataReceived(function (oEvent) {
                console.log("Data received:", oEvent.getParameter("data"));
              });
            
              oListBinding.requestContexts(0, 1).then(function (aContexts) {
                if (aContexts.length > 0) {
                  var oData = aContexts[0].getObject();
                  var ppm = oData.Ppm || 0;
            
                  this._oNumericContent.setValue(ppm);
                  this._oNumericContent.setScale("PPM");
                  this._oNumericContent.setIndicator("None");
            
                  var sColor, sIcon, sText;
                  var oTile = this.byId("tile");
        
                  if (ppm < 600) {
                    sColor = "Good";
                    sIcon = "sap-icon://weather-sunny";
                    sText = "Normal level";
                    oTile.setHeader("Good level");
                  } else if (ppm < 1000) {
                    sColor = "Critical";
                    sIcon = "sap-icon://temperature";
                    sText = "Moderate level";
                    oTile.setHeader("Average level");
                  } else {
                    sColor = "Error";
                    sIcon = "sap-icon://alert";
                    sText = "Dangerous level";
                    oTile.setHeader("Bad level");
                    var oAudio = new Audio("audio/alarm.mp3");
                    oAudio.play().catch(function (err) {
                      console.error("Error sound output:", err);
                    });
                  }
            
                  this._oNumericContent.setValueColor(sColor);
                  this._oNumericContent.setIcon(sIcon);
        //          this._oLevelText.setText(sText);
            
                } else {
                  this._oNumericContent.setValue(0);
                  this._oNumericContent.setScale("PPM");
                  this._oNumericContent.setValueColor("Neutral");
                  this._oNumericContent.setIcon("sap-icon://question-mark");
        //          this._oLevelText.setText("No data");
                }
              }.bind(this)).catch(function (oError) {
                MessageToast.show("Data loading error");
                console.error(oError);
              });
            },        
        
            onTilePress: function () {
              this._loadLatestData();
              MessageToast.show("Data refreshed");
            },
        
            onExit: function () {
              this._stopAutoRefresh();
            }
              
          });

});
<mvc:View controllerName="co2project.controller.Main"
 
  xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
  xmlns="sap.m"
  xmlns:core="sap.ui.core">

  <Page id="page" title="CO2 Sensor">
    <content>

      <HBox id="autoRefreshBox" alignItems="Center" justifyContent="End" class="sapUiSmallMargin">
        <Text id="autoRefreshLabel" text="Autorefresh" />
        <Switch id="autoRefreshSwitch" state="true" change="onAutoRefreshChange" />
      </HBox>

      <GenericTile
        id="tile"
        header="Loading..."
        frameType="TwoByOne"
        press="onTilePress">

        <TileContent id="tileContent">
          <VBox
            id="vboxContent"
            class="sapUiSmallMargin"
            alignItems="Center"
            justifyContent="Start">

            <NumericContent
              id="numContent"
              value="0"
              scale="PPM"
              indicator="Up"
              icon="sap-icon://alert"
              valueColor="Neutral"
              width="100%" />

          </VBox>
        </TileContent>

      </GenericTile>
    </content>
  </Page>

</mvc:View>

Let’s check the result:

It works! Success!

4. Finally, let’s create a mobile application using Android Studio.

Link for Android Studio - Android Studio 

After Android studio installation, let’s create a Project: Empty Activity

For the URLs, let’s create a file Config, similar to .env file that we prepared for Python scripts:

...and will change MainActivity file. The language is Kotlin.

package com.example.co2_app

import android.os.Bundle
import androidx.activity.ComponentActivity
import androidx.activity.compose.setContent
import androidx.compose.foundation.Image
import androidx.compose.foundation.background
import androidx.compose.foundation.layout.*
import androidx.compose.foundation.lazy.LazyColumn
import androidx.compose.foundation.lazy.items
import androidx.compose.material3.*
import androidx.compose.runtime.*
import androidx.compose.ui.Alignment
import androidx.compose.ui.Modifier
import androidx.compose.ui.graphics.Color
import androidx.compose.ui.res.painterResource
import androidx.compose.ui.text.font.FontWeight
import androidx.compose.ui.unit.dp
import androidx.compose.ui.unit.sp
import kotlinx.coroutines.*
import okhttp3.*
import okhttp3.Credentials
import org.json.JSONObject
import java.io.IOException
import java.text.SimpleDateFormat
import java.util.*
import com.example.co2_app.Config

class MainActivity : ComponentActivity() {

    private val clientId = Config.CLIENT_ID
    private val clientSecret = Config.CLIENT_SECRET
    private val tokenUrl = Config.TOKEN_URL
    private val odataUrl = Config.ODATA_URL
    private val username = Config.USERNAME
    private val password = Config.PASSWORD

    private val client: OkHttpClient
        get() = OkHttpClient()

    override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) {
        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState)
        setContent {
            CO2AppUI()
        }
    }

    @OptIn(ExperimentalMaterial3Api::class)
    @Composable
    fun CO2AppUI() {
        var co2Value by remember { mutableStateOf(0.0) }
        var isLoading by remember { mutableStateOf(false) }
        var errorMessage by remember { mutableStateOf<String?>(null) }
        var sensorDataList by remember { mutableStateOf(listOf<SensorData>()) }
        var autoRefreshEnabled by remember { mutableStateOf(false) }
        val coroutineScope = rememberCoroutineScope()

        fun getBackgroundColor(ppm: Double): Color {
            return when {
                ppm < 800 -> Color(0xFFB9F6CA)
                ppm < 1200 -> Color(0xFFFFF59D)
                ppm < 2000 -> Color(0xFFFFAB91)
                else -> Color(0xFFEF5350)
            }
        }

        fun getCO2Message(ppm: Double): String {
            return when {
                ppm < 800 -> "Air quality is good."
                ppm < 1000 -> "Slight stuffiness detected."
                ppm < 1500 -> "Air quality is poor, concentration may decrease."
                ppm < 2000 -> "Headache and drowsiness possible, consider ventilation."
                ppm < 5000 -> "High CO2 levels! Open windows to improve ventilation."
                else -> "Dangerously high CO2 level! Immediate action required."
            }
        }

        fun fetchData() {
            isLoading = true
            errorMessage = null
            coroutineScope.launch {
                try {
                    val token = getOAuthToken()
                    co2Value = getCO2Data(token)
                    sensorDataList = getSensorDataList(token)
                } catch (e: Exception) {
                    errorMessage = e.message
                } finally {
                    isLoading = false
                }
            }
        }

        LaunchedEffect(autoRefreshEnabled) {
            if (autoRefreshEnabled) {
                while (true) {
                    fetchData()
                    delay(10_000L)
                }
            }
        }

        Scaffold(
            topBar = {
                TopAppBar(
                    title = {
                        Text(
                            "CO2 Monitor",
                            fontWeight = FontWeight.Bold,
                            fontSize = 20.sp,
                            color = Color.White
                        )
                    },
                    colors = TopAppBarDefaults.mediumTopAppBarColors(containerColor = Color(0xFF333333))
                )
            },
            containerColor = getBackgroundColor(co2Value)
        ) { padding ->
            Column(
                modifier = Modifier
                    .fillMaxSize()
                    .padding(padding)
                    .padding(16.dp),
                horizontalAlignment = Alignment.CenterHorizontally
            ) {
                Image(
                    painter = painterResource(id = R.drawable.co2_logo),
                    contentDescription = "CO2 Icon",
                    modifier = Modifier
                        .size(100.dp)
                        .padding(bottom = 16.dp)
                )

                Text(
                    "Current CO2 value:",
                    style = MaterialTheme.typography.titleLarge.copy(
                        fontWeight = FontWeight.Bold,
                        fontSize = 28.sp,
                        color = Color.Black
                    )
                )

                Spacer(Modifier.height(16.dp))

                Text(
                    String.format("%.1f ppm", co2Value),
                    style = MaterialTheme.typography.headlineMedium.copy(
                        fontWeight = FontWeight.ExtraBold,
                        fontSize = 48.sp,
                        color = Color.Black
                    )
                )

                Spacer(Modifier.height(8.dp))

                Text(
                    getCO2Message(co2Value),
                    fontWeight = FontWeight.SemiBold,
                    fontSize = 18.sp,
                    color = Color.Black
                )

                Spacer(Modifier.height(24.dp))

                Row(
                    modifier = Modifier.fillMaxWidth(),
                    horizontalArrangement = Arrangement.spacedBy(16.dp, Alignment.CenterHorizontally)
                ) {
                    Button(
                        onClick = { fetchData() },
                        enabled = !isLoading,
                        colors = ButtonDefaults.buttonColors(containerColor = Color(0xFF6200EE))
                    ) {
                        if (isLoading) {
                            CircularProgressIndicator(
                                modifier = Modifier.size(20.dp),
                                color = Color.White,
                                strokeWidth = 2.dp
                            )
                        } else {
                            Text(
                                "Refresh",
                                fontWeight = FontWeight.Bold,
                                fontSize = 18.sp,
                                color = Color.White
                            )
                        }
                    }

                    Button(
                        onClick = { autoRefreshEnabled = !autoRefreshEnabled },
                        colors = ButtonDefaults.buttonColors(
                            containerColor = if (autoRefreshEnabled) Color(0xFF388E3C) else Color(0xFFD32F2F)
                        )
                    ) {
                        Text(
                            if (autoRefreshEnabled) "Auto-Refresh ON" else "Auto-Refresh OFF",
                            fontWeight = FontWeight.Bold,
                            fontSize = 18.sp,
                            color = Color.White
                        )
                    }
                }

                Spacer(Modifier.height(32.dp))

                LazyColumn(
                    modifier = Modifier
                        .fillMaxWidth()
                        .weight(1f),
                    verticalArrangement = Arrangement.spacedBy(8.dp)
                ) {
                    items(sensorDataList) { item ->
                        SensorDataRow(item)
                    }
                }

                errorMessage?.let {
                    Spacer(Modifier.height(20.dp))
                    Text("Error: $it", color = MaterialTheme.colorScheme.error)
                }
            }
        }
    }

    @Composable
    fun SensorDataRow(data: SensorData) {
        Card(
            modifier = Modifier.fillMaxWidth(),
            colors = CardDefaults.cardColors(containerColor = Color.White),
            elevation = CardDefaults.cardElevation(defaultElevation = 4.dp)
        ) {
            Row(
                modifier = Modifier.padding(12.dp),
                horizontalArrangement = Arrangement.SpaceBetween,
                verticalAlignment = Alignment.CenterVertically
            ) {
                Column {
                    Text(
                        text = "CO2: ${String.format("%.1f ppm", data.ppm)}",
                        fontWeight = FontWeight.Bold,
                        fontSize = 18.sp
                    )
                    Text(
                        text = data.formattedDate,
                        fontSize = 14.sp,
                        color = Color.DarkGray
                    )
                }
            }
        }
    }

    private suspend fun getOAuthToken(): String = withContext(Dispatchers.IO) {
        val credential = Credentials.basic(clientId, clientSecret)
        val formBody = FormBody.Builder()
            .add("grant_type", "password")
            .add("username", username)
            .add("password", password)
            .build()

        val request = Request.Builder()
            .url(tokenUrl)
            .header("Authorization", credential)
            .post(formBody)
            .build()

        val response = client.newCall(request).execute()
        if (!response.isSuccessful) throw IOException("Failed to get token: ${response.code}")

        val json = JSONObject(response.body!!.string())
        json.getString("access_token")
    }

    private suspend fun getCO2Data(token: String): Double = withContext(Dispatchers.IO) {
        val request = Request.Builder()
            .url(odataUrl)
            .header("Authorization", "Bearer $token")
            .build()

        val response = client.newCall(request).execute()
        if (!response.isSuccessful) throw IOException("Failed to get CO2 data: ${response.code}")

        val bodyString = response.body!!.string()
        val json = JSONObject(bodyString)
        val results = json.optJSONArray("value")
        if (results != null && results.length() > 0) {
            val firstEntry = results.getJSONObject(0)
            return@withContext firstEntry.optDouble("Ppm", 0.0)
        }

        return@withContext 0.0
    }

    private suspend fun getSensorDataList(token: String): List<SensorData> = withContext(Dispatchers.IO) {
        val request = Request.Builder()
            .url(odataUrl)
            .header("Authorization", "Bearer $token")
            .build()

        val response = client.newCall(request).execute()
        if (!response.isSuccessful) throw IOException("Failed to get CO2 data list: ${response.code}")

        val bodyString = response.body!!.string()
        val json = JSONObject(bodyString)
        val results = json.optJSONArray("value") ?: return@withContext emptyList()

        val list = mutableListOf<SensorData>()
        val sdfInput = SimpleDateFormat("yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss", Locale.getDefault())
        val sdfOutput = SimpleDateFormat("dd.MM.yyyy HH:mm:ss", Locale.getDefault())

        for (i in 0 until results.length()) {
            val item = results.getJSONObject(i)
            val ppm = item.optDouble("Ppm", 0.0)
            val createdAt = item.optString("CreatedAt", "")
            val date = try {
                sdfInput.parse(createdAt)
            } catch (e: Exception) {
                null
            }
            val formattedDate = if (date != null) sdfOutput.format(date) else createdAt
            list.add(SensorData(ppm, formattedDate))
        }

        list
    }

    data class SensorData(val ppm: Double, val formattedDate: String)
}

It will be a single-page application that retrieves sensor data and shows it on the mobile screen.

Don’t forget to open app/src/main/AndroidManifest.xml

and put a string “<uses-permission android:name="android.permission.INTERNET" />”

Rebuild the project: Menu – Build -> Compile All sources.

Run the project: Menu – Run -> Run app

The mobile app is functional. Data can be refreshed manually or via AutoRefresh.

So, project is fully operational. The combination of IoT, ABAP RAP, Fiori, and an Android app works seamlessly. While there are some simplifications, assumptions, and areas that are not fully optimized, the project successfully demonstrates how applications can be built using SAP cloud solutions. I hope you will find something useful here.

entry.title: Week 2 : Use Joule Chat to create ABAP RAP Application and explore the Explain feature
entry.content:
Last week we had completed the system set up and also created a package with your community ID. We had also gone through the help document on SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI Capabilities.

Now let us dive into the world of ABAP AI by creating an application where Joule will guide you during each step.

Quick Links

Please use this separate thread to ask your questions and discuss issues. 

Task 1: Create a ABAP RAP application using 'OData UI Service from Scratch’ wizard

It can be any application of your choice preferably non enterprise related to keep the challenge simple and more interesting. One field should be an amount field and another one a date field so that we can leverage J4D(Joule for Developers) to add some logic in the subsequent weeks. You should have 3 more fields which will be used to try out some of the J4D features.

Note: It is recommended to create all the fields while creating the app itself to avoid any manual coding later.

Example:- You can create a book store application consisting of fields ID, Title, Author, Price, Published Date, Stock, Discounted Price, BookAge. Here the last 3 fields can be used to try out the Joule for Developers (J4D) features. You are free to choose any dataset and logic combinations.

  1. Right click on the package ZAC_<your community ID> created and select ‘Generate ABAP Repository Objects’Generate ABAP Repository Objects’
  2. In the below popup that appears choose ‘Odata UI Service from Scratch’ and click on ‘Next’ two times.Odata UI Service from Scratch
  3. Click ‘Next’ again.Click Next
  4. In the next screen make sure you get the Joule Help to the right of Wizard popup. Here in the section ‘Ask Joule to update the fields’ can provide your prompts to create the application you like by specifying the fields and other details. Hit enter. Note: It is better to create all objects ending with your <community ID> to avoid any naming conflicts. Try to specify this in the prompt.Joule option in WizardNote: Make sure to select the option -  ‘Transactional with Draft’ highlighted in the above screenshot is selected to enable ‘Create’ option in the application getting created. For single entity sometimes it is set as ‘Without Draft’.
  5. Joule will now propose the Entity and Fields based on your prompt. Please note that we still should verify whether the suggestions are accurate for our requirements. Here you can notice that sometimes Joule suggests to change the Service Configuration to ‘Transactional Without Draft’, we can handle this in next step.Joule Field Proposal
  6. Once you are satisfied click on ‘Accept’ and you can see that the ‘Artifact Suffix’ if specified and the ‘Project Name’ gets added as shown in the below screenshot.           Here remember to change the Service Configuration to ‘Transactional with Draft’ and click ‘Next’.Joule Accept Proposal
  7. Joule lists the artifacts that will be created. Verify them again and click on ‘Next’ and then ‘Finish’ on the next screen. 
  8. Refresh the package and check all the artifacts have been created. Publish the Service Binding and preview the application.
  9. The application will open and should have a ‘Create’ button. Here you can either create a data generator class and add some simple logic to fill data / Use the ‘Create’ option in the app to create some sample records. ( Please create 4-5 entries in any case ). You can also adjust the metadata if you like.Application Created

For more information: https://help.sap.com/docs/abap-cloud/abap-development-tools-user-guide/use-odata-ui-service-from-scratch-wizard?locale=en-US

Task 2: Add a simple logic in the CDS data definition

  1. Add some simple logic in the CDS data definition for example to provide 2% discount on actual Price and fill another field say ‘DiscountedPrice’ with the discounted value for all records in the database.

    Note: The logic and examples are mentioned just for your reference and to make the purpose of the challenge easy to understand. Please feel free to use your own logic and dataset.

  2. Now that one of the fields was enhanced, you may need to recreate the draft table ending with _D### before launching the application again. If you do not face any issue you can skip this step.

    To do that, go to the Project Explorer and open the behavior definition click on the icon as shown on the picture to follow the quick fix. Save and activate the draft table.

    Recreate Draft Table

Task 3: Use Joule->Explain capability to understand code

Use ‘Explain’ capability of Joule to get detailed explanation of the code added.

Hint: Please add few lines of code like a simple CASE statement in CDS data definition to test Explain feature.

You may also watch this video to know more about how to use Joule->Explain feature.

We will be back next week with another feature of Joule AI Capability. Stay Tuned!!

entry.title: Data Integration in ABAP Cloud: Data Consumption using External Entities
entry.content:
In the previous post, we discussed data exposure using SQL services. In this post, we shift the focus to the data consumption scenario.

Imagine an ABAP Cloud system that needs to access data located elsewhere, perhaps in an SAP HANA Cloud database, or even in another ABAP system. With SQL Services, an ABAP system can itself act as a database. To support such outbound data access in a clean and cloud-compliant way, ABAP Cloud introduces a new CDS modelling artifact: CDS External Entities.

 

What Are CDS External Entities?

CDS External Entities enable outbound SQL access from an ABAP Cloud system. This means you can:

  • Access data from a remote database
  • Load and process remote data in ABAP
  • Combine remote data with local data

In ABAP Cloud, classic secondary database connections are not available. CDS External Entities are their more powerful and cloud-ready replacement.

Conceptually, a CDS External Entity is comparable to an SAP HANA virtual table; it represents a local view of a remote table or view. The remote object and its fields may have different names, which can be mapped explicitly.

CDS External Entities can be consumed in two different ways:

- Dynamic Access

In the SAP BTP ABAP environment, a CDS External Entity can be used directly in ABAP SQL. You write a SELECT statement that accesses data from a remote database, loads it, and processes it in ABAP.

- Static Access

Starting with release 2508, CDS view entities and classic CDS views can be built on top of external entities. This allows you to integrate remote data seamlessly into your CDS view modelling and treat it like local data. This feature will be used in my next blog post of the series.

Logical External Schema

Alongside CDS External Entities, there is a second important artifact: the Logical External Schema which is based on SAP HANA Smart Data Access (SDA). The key features are as follows:

  • It represents a remote system at design time.
  • It is mapped to a concrete remote source at configuration time.
  • It contains connection-related information (host, port, user, etc.).

From a runtime perspective, the ABAP system sends the query to its own HANA database, which then federates the query to the remote system using SDA.

 

How External Entities Work at Runtime

External entities allow remote data access using a new ABAP SQL syntax:

SELECT FROM <external_entity>

  PROVIDED BY <logical_external_schema>

  INTO TABLE @DATA(result).

In this flow:

  1. The ABAP application sends the SQL query to the local HANA database.
  2. HANA performs query federation using Smart Data Access.
  3. Data is retrieved from the remote database.
  4. Results are returned to the ABAP application server.

The <logical_external_schema> is a design-time artifact that points to the remote system and is bound to an actual remote source during configuration.

 

Example Scenario

Let us build an example based on the travel scenario in which

  • SAP HANA Cloud acts as the remote database.
  • The ABAP system consumes data via Dynamic CDS External Entities.

It is crucial that

  • The external entity has the same name and field structure as the remote HANA table.
  • Field names and types must match exactly (HANA is case-sensitive).

Besides, to enable the access, an outbound service and a communication scenario are required.

Design Time Steps

1. Define a Dynamic CDS External Entity

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK
@EndUserText.label: 'external entity for travel'
define external entity ZI_EE_TRAVEL_000 external name TRAVEL
{
  key TravelID      : abap.numc(8)                external name "TravelID";
      AgencyID      : abap.numc(6)                external name AgencyID;
      CustomerID    : abap.numc(6)                external name CustomerID;
      @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'CurrencyCode'
      BookingFee    : abap.curr(16,2)             external name BookingFee;
      @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'CurrencyCode'
      TotalPrice    : abap.curr(16,2)             external name TotalPrice;
      CurrencyCode  : abap.cuky                   external name CurrencyCode;
      BeginDate     : abap.dats                   external name BeginDate;
      EndDate       : abap.dats                   external name EndDate;
      Description   : abap.sstring(1024)          external name Description;
      OverallStatus : abap.char(1)                external name OverallStatus;
      CreatedBy     : abp_creation_user           external name CreatedBy;
      CreatedAt     : abp_creation_tstmpl         external name CreatedAt;
      LastChangedBy : abp_locinst_lastchange_user external name LastChangedBy;
}
with federated data 
provided at runtime;

This entity models the structure of the remote table or view.

2. Define a Logical External Schema

This acts as a placeholder for the remote database and is referenced by the external entity.

3. Create Outbound Service and Communication Scenario

  • Create an SQL type outbound service for the logical external schema
  • Create a communication scenario and assign the outbound service to it. Then save and publish the scenario locally.

This enables administrators to configure access to the remote system

4. Consume the External Entity in ABAP SQL

Create a class and write ABAP SQL code

Select * from my external entity provided by my remote into table my local table

CLASS zcl_travel_consume_000 DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_oo_adt_classrun .
  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_travel_consume_000 IMPLEMENTATION.
METHOD if_oo_adt_classrun~main.
     data: travel type table of zi_ee_travel_000.

    select * from zi_ee_travel_000 provided by z_travel_remote_db_000 into table @Travel.

    out->write( travel ).
    endmethod.

ENDCLASS.

In summary, what happens at runtime is as follows

  • The ABAP system sends the query to its HANA database
  • HANA SDA forwards the query to the remote database
  • Data is fetched and returned to the ABAP application
  • The local internal table is filled with remote data

Now we need to establish the connection between our development system and the SAP HANA Cloud database.

 

Configuration Time Steps

1. Create Communication Arrangement

In the SAP Fiori Launchpad:

  • Create a communication arrangement for the communication scenario you published in design time.

2. Maintain a communication system for the remote database

  • Activate Remote SQL-Access
  • Choose HANA ODBC as the SDA adapter
  • Provide host, port, driver, and additional connection settings
    (These details are typically provided by the system administrator)

save the communication system.

3. Maintain Remote Schema Information

In the communication arrangement:

  • Specify the schema name of the remote HANA database
  • Save the configuration and check whether the connection is successful

4. Prepare the Remote HANA Database

In Visual Studio Code and using SAP HANA Database Explorer:

  • Create a table that matches the CDS External Entity exactly
  • Ensure field names, types, and sizes match as well

Remember that SAP HANA is case-sensitive.

What Happens Behind the Scenes

When you execute the ABAP SQL statement:

  • The ABAP SQL layer resolves the external entity and logical external schema
  • HANA automatically:
    • Creates a remote source
    • Creates the required virtual table
  • Query federation is handled transparently

In this sense, CDS External Entities act as a smart abstraction that automatically manages HANA remote sources and virtual tables via ABAP design-time objects.

Press F9, and the data is returned as expected.
